# MAIN ARTICLE

www.afm-journal.de


# V2O5Nanowires with an Intrinsic Peroxidase-Like Activity


Rute   André  ,   Filipe   Natálio  ,   Madalena   Humanes  ,   Jana   Leppin  ,   Katja   Heinze  ,   Ron   Wever  , H.-C.   Schröder  ,   Werner E.   G. Müller  ,   and   Wolfgang   Tremel  *

V2O5nanowires exhibit an intrinsic catalytic activity towards classical peroxidase substrates such as2,2-azino-bis(3-ethylbenzothiazoline-6-sulfonic acid) (ABTS) and3,3,5,5,-tetramethylbenzdine (TMB) in the presence of H2O2 . These V2O5nanowires show an optimum reactivity at a pH of4.0and the catalytic activity is dependent on the concentration. The Michaelis-Menten kinetics of the ABTS oxidation over these nanowires reveals a behavior similar to that of their natural vanadium-dependent haloperoxidase (V-HPO) counterparts. The V2O5nanowires mediate the oxidation of ABTS in the presence of H2O2with a turnover frequency ( k cat ) of2.5 × 10 3s -1 . The K M values of the V2O5nanowires for ABTS oxidation (0.4 µ M  ) and for H2O2 (2.9 µ M  ) at a pH of4.0are signifi  cantly smaller than those reported for horseradish peroxidases (HRP) and V-HPO indicating a higher affi  nity of the substrates for the V2O5nanowire surface. Based on the kinetic parameters and similarity with vanadium-based complexes a mechanism is proposed where an intermediate metastable peroxo complex is formed as the fi  rst catalytic step. The nanostructured vanadium-based material can be re-used up to10times and retains its catalytic activity in a wide range of organic solvents (up to90%) making it a promising mimic of peroxidase catalysts.


# 1. Introduction


Vanadium (V) oxide is a well-studied material with a broad range of applications such as in electrochromic/electrooptic devices, [1]

R.   André  ,   Dr. F.   Natálio  ,   J.   Leppin  ,   Prof. K.   Heinze  ,   Prof. W .   T remel Institute for Inorganic Chemistry

Johannes Gutenberg-University

Duesbergweg10-14, D-55099Mainz, Germany

E-mail:   tremel@uni-mainz.de

Prof. M.   Humanes

Center of Chemistry and Biochemistry

Faculty of Sciences

Building C8, Campo Grande

1749-016Lisbon, Portugal

Prof. R.   Wever

Van't Hoff Institute for Molecular Sciences

Universiteit van Amsterdam

Nieuwe Achtergracht129, 1018WS Amsterdam, Holland

Prof. H.-C.   Schröder  ,   Prof. W. E. G.   Müller Institute for Physiological Chemistry

Dept. for Applied Molecular Genetics

Duesbergweg10-14, D-55099Mainz, Germany

DOI: 10.1002/adfm.201001302

electrodes for lithium batteries, [2] catalysis, [3] gas sensors, [4] electrochemistry, [5] and photocatalytic  reactions. [6] From  the  many different compositions of vanadium oxide, V2O5is the most widely studied vanadium oxide, especially because of its unique layered structure, which also makes it a very good intercalation material. [7]  Compared to bulk  vanadium  oxide,  defi  ned  nano-  and microstructures present signifi  cantly better performances in energy-storage devices or sensing  devices. [8] However,  the  catalytic activity of nanostructured V2O5has hardly been studied in contrast to the corresponding bulk unstructured material. The presence of vanadium in biological systems is  well  known.  The  metal  has  been  found in  high  concentrations  in  several  marine and terrestrial organisms, such as ascidians (tunicates),  algae,  or  fungi. [9] In  some  of these organisms its role is still unclear, but in most cases vanadium is associated with the active center or is part of a co-factor in enzymes such as vanadium haloperoxidase (VHPO) or vanadium nitrogenase. [10]

Vanadium haloperoxidases (VHPOs) are enzymes that catalyze  the  oxidation  of  halides  to  the  corresponding  hypohalous acids using hydrogen peroxide (H2O2 ) as the oxidant. When suitable nucleophilic acceptors are present, halogenated compounds are  formed.  The  presence  of  the  haloperoxidases  in  organisms is thought to be related to the production of halogenated compounds with biocidal activity. VHPOs have been used in a variety  of  biotransformations, not only in halogenations [11] but also  in  sulfoxidation, [12] showing a remarkable stereoselectivity and  regiospecifi  city. [13] Haloperoxidases  also  showed  activity towards classical peroxidase substrates, [14] in  the  same  fashion as the well-known horseradish peroxidase (HRP) and are extensively  used  in  biochemistry,  biological  assays, [15] as  well  as  in wastewater treatment (i.e., to remove aromatic compounds). [16] Several mimetic compounds for VHPOs have been discovered, consisting  mostly  of  vanadium  organometallic  complexes  or peroxocomplexes, [17] which  exhibit  good  effi  ciency  and  selectivity  in  several  oxidation  reactions  such  as  halogenation, [18] sulfoxidation, [19] epoxidation,  hydroxylation  of  alkenes, [20] and oxidation of primary and secondary alcohols. [21] The active species  in  these  complexes has been identifi  ed as oxoperoxovanadium (V) complexes, and some structural characterization has been carried out. [22]  However, the use of natural or recombinant enzymes, or biomimetic vanadium compounds brings up issues regarding  the  cost  of  production  and  reaction  conditions.  For

example, the enzymes have the disadvantage of a short half life and usually need very specifi  c reaction conditions. [23] Although some VHPOs can withstand organic solvent concentrations of up to60%, some biotransformations cannot be performed in the presence of water. [24]  In addition, the production of recombinant enzymes  is  not  always  straightforward,  as  would  be  required for industrial applications, and, moreover, the stability is always an issue. Some vanadium catalysts (vanadium complexes) used nowadays,  such  as  bulk  vanadium  pentoxide,  are  required  in high  amounts [25] during  the  catalytic  reaction  and  require,  for example,  high  amounts  of  acid. [26] These  catalysts  cannot  be reused as they degrade during reaction, and their immobilization in polymer or zeolite supports is not so easy. [27]

For this work, we studied V2O5nanowires that were obtained via hydrothermal synthesis for their ability to catalyze peroxidative  reactions  in  aqueous  media  and  other  solvents.  The  reaction catalyzed by these nanowires followed a Michaelis-Menten behavior showing an excellent catalytic activity superior in comparison to its natural counterparts, such as V-HPO and HRP. The kinetic parameters were also obtained and compared with peroxidase  and  haloperoxidase  enzymes.  The  new  nanostructured vanadium-based material is re-usable up to10times and retains its catalytic activity in different organic solvents (up to90% v/v), making it a promising mimic of peroxidase catalysts.


# 2. Results and Discussion



# 2.1. Synthesis and Characterization of V2O5Nanowires


V2O5nanowires  were  prepared  using  a  hydrothermal  method as  described  elsewhere. [28] Briefl  y,  for  the  synthesis  of  V2O5nanowires the water-soluble vanadium (IV) oxosulfate (VOSO4 ) was  oxidized  with  potassium  bromate  (KBrO3 )  by  stirring  for30min at room temperature. After lowering the pH using nitric acid, the reaction was kept at180 ° C for24h. After cooling to room temperature and extensive washing, the dark-yellow precipitate was dried in an oven at80 ° C overnight and analyzed by high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) and X-ray diffraction (XRD).

The powder XRD pattern of the V2O5nanowires in Figure1A shows the presence of orthorhombic V2O5 (Shcherbianite, PDF003-0207).  Transmission  electron  microscopy  (TEM)  images (Figure1B) showed freshly synthesized V2O5nanowires to have a  variable  size  from500nm  up  to  several µ m in  length  and100nm in width. Figure1C shows a high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) image of such a crystalline nanowire  with  lattice  fringes  of0.516nm  corresponding  to orthorhombic V2O5 . The fast Fourier transform (FFT) indicates

Figure2  . V2O5nanowires  (A)  catalyze,  in  the  presence  of  H2O2 ,  the  oxidation of various classical peroxidase substrates (HA): 2,2 ′ -Azino-bis(3-ethylbenzothiazoline-6-sulfonic acid) (ABTS) (B) and3,3,5,5,-tetramethylbenzdine (TMB) (C); displaying an intrinsic peroxidase-like activity.

single-crystalline  behavior  (Figure1C,  inset).  Complementary analytical data on V2O5nanowires using energy-dispersive analysis of X-rays (EDX) confi  rmed that only vanadium and oxygen were present (Figure1D  ).


# 2.2. Peroxidase-Like Activity


The V2O5nanowires are capable of catalyzing typical peroxidase reactions, as shown in Figure2 , using both chromogenic heme peroxidase substrates2,2-azino-bis(3-ethylbenzothiazoline6-sulfonic  acid)  (ABTS)  (Figure2A)  and3,3,5,5,-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) (Figure2B)  in  the  presence  of  H2O2to  produce  a  green  (maximum  absorbance405nm)  and  blue  color (maximum absorbance652nm) reaction, respectively. Initially, these reactions were carried out by incubating0.02mg mL -1of V2O5nanowires with the substrates (0.68m  M  ) and H2O2 (1m  M  ) for60s at room temperature in a buffered solution (100mM sodium acetate buffer, pH4.0). This fi  rst approach indicates that these V2O5nanowires have an intrinsic peroxidase-like activity towards these substrates (Figure2  ). The reactions were also carried out in the absence of nanowires and at this pH value no signifi  cant unspecifi  c oxidation reaction was observed (data not shown). Additional control experiments using V2O5nanowires in  the  absence  of  either  the  electron  donor  (ABTS/TMB)  or H2O2showed that no oxidative reaction occurs indicating that both  components  are  required  for  the  reaction  to  occur  as  is also the case for V-HPO. [29]  For comparison purposes, the oxidation activity towards ABTS of commercially available bulk V2O5was also tested. TEM analysis of bulk V2O5shows an unstructured (Figure S1A) and/or agglomerated material (Figure S1B) composed of crystals with variable size. Although the XRD pattern obtained from bulk V2O5matches the XRD pattern of V2O5nanowires (Figure S1C) no ABTS oxidation activity of bulk V2O5was observed under conditions used in the above experiments, thus highlighting the importance of the nanostructure for the catalytic properties (Figure S1D).


# 2.3. The Activity Is Related to the Nano-V2O5and Not to Free Vanadate in Solution from Leaching Processes


In order to demonstrate that the intrinsic peroxidase-like catalytic  properties  of  the  V2O5nanowires  towards  ABTS  result

www.afm-journal.de from the surface properties of the nanostructure and not from free orthovanadate anions, VO4 3 -(10 µ M  ) was incubated under the same experimental conditions (e.g., 100m  M acetate buffer pH4.0, 0.68m  M of ABTS, 1m  M H2O2 ,  60s,  RT).  Additionally,  experiments  were  carried  out  whereby  the  supernatant of  an  aqueous  solution  of  V2O5nanowires  (0.02mg  mL -1 ) was  tested  towards  the  ABTS  oxidation  reaction.  No  reaction  occurred  in  the  presence  of  VO4 3 -(Figure  S2A)  nor  in the case of the V2O5aqueous supernatant (Figure S2B), thus ruling out the possibility that the intrinsic peroxidase-like catalytic  activity  is  related  to  an  ion-leaching  process  from  the nanowires.


# 2.4. Kinetic Parameters and Reaction Mechanism


One of the most interesting features about the V2O5nanowires is  their  ability  to  catalyze  reactions  under  mild  conditions, which  so  far  has  only  been  attributed  to  enzymes.  Recently, it  was  reported  that  vanadium  haloperoxidase  (V-HPO)  (e.g., recombinant  vanadium  chloroperoxidase  -  VCPO)  from  the fungus C.  inaequalis catalyzes  the  oxidation  of  ABTS  in  the presence of H2O2 . This striking similarity, prompted us to characterize  the  peroxidase-like  activity  of  the  V2O5nanowires  for ABTS  in  more  detail  ( Figure3 ).  In  a  typical  experiment,  the V2O5oxidation  activity  was  determined  by  monitoring  of  the absorbance at405nm (which increases because of ABTS * + formation) for300s at25 ° C in acetate buffer (100mM pH4.0) for varying concentrations of the V2O5nanowires ( < 0.04mg mL -1 ) with  respect  to  ABTS  (0.68m  M  )  and  H2O2 (1m  M  ).  From Figure3A it can be observed that the ABTS oxidation rate catalyzed by the V2O5nanowires was dependent on the concentration of the nanowires. A control experiment with orthovanadate anions (VO4 3 -, 10 µ M  ) in the presence of both H2O2 (1m  M  ) and ABTS (0.68m  M  ) showed no reaction demonstrating once more that the ABTS oxidation reaction is exclusively due to the V2O5nanowires and not to a leaching process of vanadate ions into the solution.  The  effect  of  pH  on  the  peroxidase-like  activity  of the V2O5nanowires (0.02mg mL -1 )  was measured by varying the  pH  from3to10,  whereas  keeping  the  ABTS  and  H2O2concentrations constant. Interestingly, the catalytic activity of  the  V2O5nanowires  shows  a  sharp  pH  optimum  at  pH4 (Figure3B) indicating that the one-electron oxidation of ABTS is  only  mediated  under  slightly  acidic  conditions,  as  has  also been reported for V-CPO from C. inaequalis . [14]  At this pH and in the absence of nanoparticles an unspecifi  c reaction between ABTS (0.68m  M  ) and H2O2 (1m  M  )  was not observed (data not shown).

The  mechanism  of  the  peroxidase-like  catalytic  activity  of the  V2O5nanowires  was  further  investigated  using  steadystate  kinetics.  From  previous  control  experiments,  we  showed that  ABTS  oxidation  catalysis  mediated  by  V2O5nanowires  is dependent on the substrate concentration (ABTS and H2O2 ) as is  also  observed in its natural counterparts, such as HRP and V-HPO.  [ 13 , 30 ] In order to get a better insight into this nanowire/ substrate  dependency,  several  experiments  were  performed whereby the concentration of either ABTS or H2O2was varied while  keeping  the  other  concentration  constant.  The  concentration  of  the  V2O5nanowires  was  kept  constant  in  all  these

Figure3  . Dependency of ABTS oxidation activity on the V2O5nanowire concentration. The rate of the reaction was determined by monitoring the increase of absorbance at405nm, which is related to the formation of ABTS ∗ + , for300s at25 ° C. A) The V2O5oxidation activity was determined in acetate buffer (100m  M pH4.0) using a nanowire concentration ranging from0up to0.04mg mL -1 , ABTS (0.68m  M  ) and H2O2 (1m  M  ) were added to initiate the reaction. B) The pH dependency of the oxidation activity of V2O5using buffer composition with different pH values. Assay conditions: V2O5nanowires (0.02mg mL -1 ), ABTS (0.68m  M  ) and H2O2 (1m  M  ).

experiments ( Figure4A and B). We observed that the oxidation reaction catalyzed by the V2O5nanowires follows a MichaelisMenten  behavior  towards  both  components,  ABTS  and  H2O2 (Figure4A and B). The kinetic parameters for ABTS and H2O2were determined at the pH optimum of4.0. The Vmax obtained was0.2807M s -1 ,  from which it was determined that the V2O5nanowires  mediate  the  oxidation  of  ABTS  in  the  presence  of H2O2with a turnover frequency ( k cat )  of2.5 × 10 3s -1 .  The k cat was determined as

By using the Lineweaver-Burk linearization process (Figure4C,D)  a  value  for K M ABTS of around0.4 µ M and K M H2O2of  about2.9 µ M  were  found.  When  we  compare  the enzyme kinetics to this heterogeneously mediated catalysis step, the kinetic parameters show that H2O2has a smaller K M value (2.9 µ M  )  than  the K M  value  previously  determined  for  V-CPO (about100 µ M  ). [31]  This suggests that H2O2might have a higher affi  nity for the surface of the V2O5nanowires than for HRP and V-HPO at pH4.0 [ 32 , 33 ]   because of the presence of more 'active sites'  on  the  surface  of  the  nanowires  compared  to  V-CPO, which only has one vanadate (VO4 3 -) ion at the active center.

By  analogy  with  vanadium  inorganic  complexes  that  have been  shown  to  mediate  the  oxidation  of  a  variety  of  organic compounds through an oxidation mechanism with the formation of a peroxo intermediate, we propose that ABTS oxidation mediated by V2O5nanowires also involves the formation of an intermediate peroxo surface complex.


# 2.5. A Chemical Model of the Reaction


A possible catalytic mechanism mediated by the V2O5nanowires is  proposed after analyzing our experimental results based on the layered V2O5orthorhombic structure ( Figure5A). [34] These layers are made up of neutral coordinatively saturated vanadium cations and are stacked in the [001] direction. In contrast, the (110) surface is formed by the cleavage of covalent V-O bonds at  the  surface,  thus  resulting  in  coordinatively  unsaturated  V and O atoms at its side faces. The (001) surface is assumed to be responsible for the selective oxidation of hydrocarbons such as  toluene,  whereas  the  reactive  (110)  surface  predominantly results in total oxidation. This surface is easily hydroxylated and as a result contains both Brönsted acid and base sites.

The exposed (010) lattice planes of the V2O5nanowires display a  vanadium coordination geometry similar to that of the active site of V-HPO, [35] and thus the peroxidase-like catalytic activity displayed by the V2O5nanowires may relate to the structure of these surface sites. In this model, the V atoms in the (010) plane act as Lewis acid sites, whereas the electron lone pairs of the bridging oxygen atoms behave as Lewis base sites where the nucleophilic addition of oxygen can occur. Consequently, the V2O5nanowires are  supposed  to  react  in  an  initial  step  with  H2O2 (Figure5B) forming an intermediate peroxo species. Structural data of this peroxo species are available in detail for V-HPO [36] and organometallic vanadium (V) complexes. [37] In the latter case the formation of the peroxo species is only favored at very acidic pH values. The formation of these peroxo species is considered an essential step in the oxidation activity. [38]   However, the optimal pH value (ca.  pH4) for the oxidation activity of these V2O5nanowires is similar to that found for V-HPO. In a subsequent step, the ABTS substrate binds to the vanadium peroxo species by a nucleophilic attack, thus allowing the oxidation reaction of ABTS to form the ABTS ∗ + species. As hydrogen peroxide is a two-electron oxidant another ABTS molecule is required to allow its regeneration, as is found in the natural enzyme (V-HPO). [14]

Two  different  types  of  oxygen  can  be  distinguished  on  the (001) surface. The fi  rst  type  are  terminal  vanadyl  oxygen  sites and the second type is the bridging oxygen that connects either two or three vanadium centers. There is a higher electron density  on  the  oxygen  atoms  with  the  higher  number  of  cation neighbors,  that  is,  because  of  the π -back  donation  the  charge on the bridging oxygen atoms is signifi  cantly larger than that on  the  terminal  vanadyl  oxygen  atoms.  On  the  other  hand, heterolytic  chemical  sorption  might  occur  through  Brönsted acid-base interactions in the (100) and (001) planes.

Figure4  . Steady-state kinetic study of V2O5nanowires. Michaelis-Menten curve fi  t for A) variation of ABTS concentration (0-0.68m  M  ) and keeping the H2O2 (1m  M  ) and V2O5nanowire (0.02mg mL -1 ) concentration constant and B) variation of H2O2concentration (0-1m  M  ) and maintaining ABTS (0.68m  M  ) and V2O5nanowire (0.02mg mL -1   ) concentration constant. The formation of ABTS ∗ + at405nm was monitored for300s at25 ° C. Corresponding Lineweaver-Burk linearization for C) ABTS and D) H2O2values.

Reactive  oxygen  species  adsorbed  on  oxide  surfaces  are important intermediates in both total and selective catalytic oxidation reactions. [38] In  particular, the catalytic oxidation of several organic compounds, such as alkanes, for example, by H2O2over TiO2or V2O5has been investigated to understand the role of the active peroxo or peroxoacid species in the decomposition mechanism.  [39] A key question in oxidative catalysis is whether a  reactive  surface-oxygen species is used to activate a covalent bond of the substrate or whether it is used for the addition of oxygen  to  reactive  surface  intermediates  to  form  oxygenated products. The EPR spectrum in Figure6is  characteristic  of  a superoxide  (O2 -)  type  radical  anion.  The  formal  assignment of the signal to an O2 -type species can be made based on the characteristic g tensor elements for the radical anion stabilized on metal oxides. [40]  Furthermore, because the g zz component is sensitive to the crystal-fi  eld environment, the g values of2.020, 2.020, and2.005observed in Figure6are consistent with those expected  for  O2 -stabilized  at  a  V5 + site.  As  a  control  experiment.  a  dispersion  of  V2O5nanowires  was  analyzed  and  no

EPR  signal  was  observed  as  expected  for  vanadium  (V)  (data not shown).


# 2.6. Catalyst Re-Utilization and Organic Solvents Catalysis


In order to test the re-utilization of the V2O5nanowires a series of  catalytic  cycles  were  carried  out.  Basically,  to  a  solution  of0.02mg  mL -1of  V2O5nanowires  prepared  in  acetate  buffer (100m  M  ,  pH4.0),  both  substrates  ABTS (0.68m  M  )  and  H2O2 (1m  M  )  were  added  and  the  absorbance  at405nm  measured (ABTS ∗ + formation).  Subsequently,  the  nanowires  were  recovered, centrifuged, and fresh substrates were added. Figure7A shows that after10cycles  the  V2O5nanowires  partially  retain their  catalytic  activity  with  some  decrease  after  each  cycle. Between each catalytic step an average decay in activity of5.8% is  observed. Still, this highlights the excellent catalytic properties of the V2O5nanowires. Because of their versatility and easy recovery  a  wider  applicability  of  this  nanomaterial  for  larger

Figure5  . A) Single layer from the V2O5structure. In the V2O5nanowires these  layers  are  stacked  along  [001],  and  wires  extend  along  the  [100] direction.  A  view  of  the  exposed  (110)  planes  shows  a  great  similarity with the V-HPO active sites. B) Proposed mechanism for the formation of ABTS with the formation of a vanadium peroxo complex intermediate and oxidation  attack  of  ABTS  and  release  of  the  product  (ABTS ∗ + ).  As hydrogen peroxide is a two-electron oxidant, another molecule of ABTS is required for regeneration of the V2O5nanowires leading to the formation of a new product (ABTS ∗ + ).

Linewidth = 3.0mT,  approximately82% superoxide and18% vanadyl ( ± 5%). [41]

Figure7  . Re-utilization  of  V2O5nanowires  in  the  oxidation  reaction  of ABTS in the presence of hydrogen peroxide. A) Catalytic activity of V2O5nanowires over various cycles of re-utilization using identical reaction conditions and a constant oxidation rate. B) TEM image of V2O5nanowires after  the10th  reaction  cycle  (0.68m  M  ABTS,  1m  M  H2O2in100m  M acetate buffer, pH4.0). No surface erosion can be observed but the formation of an organic layer is clearly present. Scale bar: 100nm. (The black arrow indicates a surface layer.)

scale  application  seems  possible.  After  the10catalytic  cycles, the V2O5nanowires were transferred to a copper grid and analyzed by TEM. A representative image in Figure7B shows that the  nanowires  remained intact,  although  an  apparent  layer  of organic matter seems to cover their surface. This layer might be due to reaction by-products that are adsorbed on the surface of  the  nanowires  decreasing  the  number  of  'active  sites'  and resulting in the observed decrease in activity after each cycle.

However, in order to determine the infl  uence of this organic layer  on  the  catalytic  properties  of  the  nanomaterial  the  V2O5nanowires were intensively washed  with different organic

www.MaterialsViews.com solvents  (hexane,  acetone,  and  ethanol  in  this  order)  after  the10th catalytic cycle and the peroxidase activity was assayed. TEM and kinetic analysis confi  rmed that the organic layer is responsible  for  the  decay  in  activity.  The  TEM  images  of  the  V2O5nanowires  after  the10th  cycle  and  organic  solvent  washing show partially clean surfaces (Figure S3B) when compared with the  as-prepared  nanowires  (Figure  S3A).  The  kinetic  studies also  revealed  an  increased  catalytic  activity  (Figure  S3C)  for the washed V2O5nanowires in comparison with the unwashed samples. Moreover, the catalytic activity of the washed samples showed a decay of only8% when compared to that of the as-prepared  samples,  indicating  that  surface  adhesion  of  impurities may well be the responsible factor for the decrease in catalytic activity.

The most interesting applications of these V2O5nanowires, as  well  as  V-HPO,  are  in  the  industrial  and  pharmaceutical fi  elds.  V-HPO  was  reported  to  be  very  stable  in  organic  solvents, retaining up to70% of its original activity. [42] Similarly, the  ABTS  oxidation  catalyzed  by  these  V2O5nanowires  was carried out in different organic solvents. However, because of the low solubility of ABTS in those organic solvents, all assays were performed in a mixture of90% organic solvent and10% water.  None  of  the  organic  solvents  mixtures  led  to  a  nonspecifi  c  reaction  between  ABTS  and  H2O2 (data  not  shown) confi  rming that the catalytic activity is strictly due to the V2O5nanowires. Figure8shows  that  the  oxidation  reactions  carried  out  in  ethanol  (90%)  and  isopropanol  (90%)  have  very similar  profi  les  compared  to  the  control  reaction  performed in  a  buffered  solution.  However,  the  oxidation  rate  increased

Figure8  . The effect of organic solvents (ethanol-EtOH, isopropanol, and acetonitrile) in a ratio with water of90:10% (v/v) on the ABTS oxidation activity mediated by V2O5nanowires. For comparison purposes, the reaction carried out in acetate buffer (100m  M  , pH4.0) is also shown. The reactions were carried out using0.68m  M ABTS, 1m  M H2O2 , and different concentrations of V2O5nanowires (0-0.04mg mL -1 ) (prepared in90% organic solvents) and the formation of ABTS ∗ + was spectrophometrically monitored at405nm.

www.afm-journal.de

2-fold when the reaction was carried out in acetonitrile (90%). It  has  been  shown  that  formation  of  transition-metal  peroxocomplexes occurs more easily in aprotic environments as the stability of the reduced form of the metal complex is promoted due to better solvation, which is not the case for proton-donor solvents. [43] In  summary,  our  fi  ndings  are  in  agreement  with the proposed catalytic mechanism in which an essential peroxo complex is formed in the fi  rst step.


# 3. Conclusions


V2O5nanowires with a variable length between500nm and a few micrometers and a width of100nm showed an intrinsic catalytic activity towards classical peroxidase substrates, such as  ABTS and TMB, in the presence of H2O2 .  Similar  to  the vanadium  haloperoxidases  (V-HPO)  these  V2O5nanowires show optimal activity in slightly acidic environments (pH4.0) with their catalytic activity being dependent on the V2O5concentration.  The  kinetics  of  the  ABTS  oxidation  were  determined  and  fi  tted  according  to  the  Michaelis-Menten  model displaying a behavior very similar to its natural counterpart, V-HPO. The obtained Km value for ABTS was approximately0.4 µ M and that for H2O2was approximately2.9 µ M at a pHw of4.0.  These  values  are  signifi  cantly  smaller  than  those reported for HRP and V-HPO indicating a stronger affi  nity of the substrates to the surface of the nanowires. Moreover, the turnover frequency ( k cat )  was determined to be2.5 × 10 3s -1 . Based on these kinetic parameters and in analogy with other inorganic  complexes  amechanism  was  proposed  where  an intermediate  metastable  peroxo  complex  is  formed  during the  fi  rst  catalytic  step  leading  to  the  one-electron  oxidation reaction of ABTS.

We  conclude  that  the  V2O5nanowires  mimic  peroxidase enzymes. At the same time, they feature good stability in different organic solvent mixtures (up to90% v/v) and they can be re-utilized without signifi  cant loss of catalytic activity.


# 4. Experimental Section


Synthesis of Divanadium Pentoxide (V2O5 ) Nanowires: V2O5nanowires were synthesized as described elsewhere, [28]  with minor adjustments. In brief, 8mmol of VOSO4 . n H2O (Alfa Aesar, purity > 99.9%) and5mmol of  KBrO3 (Sigma  Aldrich,  purity > 98%)  were  dissolved  in30mL  of distilled  water  and  stirred  for30min  at  room  temperature.  Nitric  acid ( Trace SELECT  ® Ultra, for trace analysis, ≥ 65%,  Fluka  Chemie  AG, Basel,  Switzerland)  was  added  dropwise under stirring until pH2was reached.  The  solution  was  transferred  to  a  tefl  on-lined  stainless  steel autoclave,  which  was  maintained  at180 ° C  for24h.  After  cooling  to room temperature  the  solution  was  fi  ltered  and  washed  several  times with distilled water and ethanol. The dark-yellow precipitate was dried in an oven at80 ° C overnight and analyzed by high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) and X-ray diffraction (XRD).

Physical Characterization : The TEM images were produced using a Philips420instrument with an acceleration voltage of120kV or a Philips TECNAI F30electron  microscope  (fi  eld-emission  gun,  300kV  extraction  voltage). X-ray powder diffraction was carried out in θ /2 θ refl  ection geometry using a  Siemens  D8power  diffractometer  equipped  with  a  position-sensitive detector.  The  data  was  collected  using  Cu  K α radiation  at  an  operating potential of40kV and a current of40mA, and analyzed using EVA software.


# www.afm-journal.de


Peroxidase-like  Catalytic  Activity  of  V2O5Nanowires :  The  peroxidaselike  activity  of  freshly  synthesized  V2O5nanowires  was  determined spectrophotometrically  by  measuring  the  formation  of  ABTS ∗ + from ABTS at405nm ( ε = 36.0m  M -1cm -1 )  using  a  multi-well  plate  reader (Titertek  Multiscan  PLUS,  MKII,  Rheinbach,  Germany).  Typically,  the ABTS oxidation activity was measured in100m  M sodium acetate buffer (pH4.0)  in  the  presence  of  V2O5nanowires  (0-0.04mg  mL -1 )  using0.68m  M ABTS  (Cat.  No.  102946,  Boehringer  Mannheim  GmbH,  Germany) during300s  at25 ° C.  Hydrogen  peroxide  (1m  M  )  (Cat.  No.  8070.1, ROTIPURAN  ®  p.a., ISO, stabilized, Carl Roth GmbH & Co.KG Karlsruhe, Germany) was added to start the reaction, unless otherwise specifi  ed. As a  control,  bulk  V2O5 (0.02mg  mL -1 )  (Cat.  No.  221899,  Sigma-Aldrich, Stenheim,  Germany),  a  solution  of  sodium  orthovanadate  (Na3VO4 , 10 µ M  )  (Cat.  No.  450243,  Sigma-Aldrich,  Stenheim,  Germany),  and  a supernatant  derived  from  an  aqueous  solution  of  V2O5 (0.02g  mL -1 ) were used under otherwise identical reaction conditions.

Determination of Kinetic Parameters : The steady-state kinetics were performed by varying the concentration of V2O5nanowires (0-0.04mg mL -1 ), H2O2 (0-1m  M  ), or ABTS (0-0.68m  M  ) one at a time. The  reaction  was  carried  out  in100m  M  acetate  buffer  (pH4.0)  and monitored spectrophotometrically for300s by measuring the formation of  ABTS ∗ + from  ABTS  at405nm  ( ε = 36.0m  M -1cm -1 )  using  a  multiwell plate reader (Titertek Multiscan PLUS, MKII, Rheinbach, Germany). The  kinetic  curves  were  adjusted  to  the  Michaelis-Menten  model  and Lineaweaver-Burk linearizations were performed using Sigma-Plot software with module enzyme kinetics (Systat Software Inc.).

pH Measurements : The activity of the V2O5nanowires at different pH values was performed using the same conditions as above, except three different buffer compositions for the different pH ranges were employed. The reaction was carried out with0.02mg mL -1of V2O5to which ABTS (0.68m  M  ) and H2O2  (1m  M  ) were added. Between pH3to6, 100m  M of sodium acetate buffer was used; for pH7, 10m  M of Tris-Cl buffer was used, and for pH8 -10, 10m  M of carbonate buffer was used. The pH of the different buffers was adjusted using a pH meter (pH-Meter PCE-228, PCE Deutschland GmbH, Meschede, Germany).

Electron  Paramagnetic  Resonance  (EPR) Measurements :  To40mg  of the as-prepared V2O5nanowires, distilled water (500 µ L) was added until the sample was completely soaked. At room temperature, H2O2 (100 µ L of30% H2O2   ) was added to the sample in order to form the superoxo species. The sample was quenched immediately with liquid nitrogen and the spectrum was recorded. The measurements were carried out using a  Magnetec  MS300spectrometer  (X-band)  with  a  frequency  counter Hewlett  Packard5340A  at  a  microwave  frequency  of9.39GHz.  As  a control experiment the V2O5nanowires (40mg) were analyzed under the same conditions but in the presence of distilled water.

'Re-utilization' Assays :  The  re-utilization  of  the  catalyst  was  tested using a scale-up of the reaction, in which5mg of V2O5nanowires were used  for  the  fi  nal  concentration  of0.02mg  mL -1 .  The  reactions  were carried out in100m  M acetate buffer (pH4.0), using0.68m  M of ABTS, and H2O2 (1m  M  )  was  added  to  start  the  reaction.  The  absorbance  at405nm was measured using a Cary5G UV-vis-NIR spectrophotometer (Varian, Inc., Palo Alto, CA, USA) after which the catalyst was spun by centrifugation (13 000rpm, 25 ° C for20min). The V2O5nanowires were washed with MilliQ water, and a new reaction cycle (a total of10) was performed following the same procedure. In order to recover the catalytic activity of the V2O5nanowires (after the10th cycle), the nanowires were intensively washed three times with hexane,then acetone, and ethanol, and the catalytic activity was assayed as described above.

Peroxidase-like Catalytic Activity of V2O5Nanowires in Organic Solvents : The catalytic activity for the oxidation of ABTS at different concentrations of V2O5nanowires (0-0.04mg mL -1   ) was assayed under the conditions described before, except that instead of acetate buffer (100m  M  , pH4.0), a  mixture  of90%  solvent  (ethanol,  isopropanol,  acetonitrile)  and10% water was used to prepare all the solutions. As a control experiment the reaction was carried out either in the absence of ABTS or V2O5 .

Statistical Data :  All  the  experiments  concerning  the  catalytic  activity of  the  V2O5nanowires  were  performed  in  triplicate  and  the  data  was analyzed using the paired Student's t -test. [43]
